subject: ap-psychology
title: Research Methods — Observational Learning and Child Development (AAQ)
stimulus:
Introduction
This study investigated whether the presence of a peer model influences the willingness of 4-year-old children to try unfamiliar foods.
Participants
Ninety-six children between the ages of 3 years 10 months and 4 years 6 months were recruited from six preschool programs in a suburban school district. Written parental consent was obtained for all participants. Children with known food allergies or developmental delays were excluded from participation, resulting in a final sample of 88 children (46 girls, 42 boys). Families were predominantly middle-income; 61% of families identified as White/European American, 18% as Hispanic/Latino, 12% as Asian/Asian American, 6% as Black/African American, and 3% as Other.
Method
Children were individually escorted to a small testing room adjacent to their classroom. In the room was a small table with two place settings. Each setting included four small cups, each containing approximately 5 grams of a different novel food item (jicama, starfruit, dried mango, and edamame). These foods were selected because pretesting confirmed that fewer than 10% of the target age group had previously encountered them.
Participants were randomly assigned to one of two conditions:
• Peer model condition: The child sat across from a confederate child of the same gender (a trained 5-year-old from a different classroom) who enthusiastically tasted all four foods before the participant was prompted to try them.
• No model condition: The child sat alone at the table and was simply asked if they would like to taste any of the foods.
In both conditions, the experimenter offered no encouragement or praise. After three minutes, the experimenter recorded how many of the four food items the child had tasted (score of 0–4) and how many grams total the child had consumed (measured via a digital scale). A follow-up measure three weeks later used parent diaries to record whether children had voluntarily requested any of the four novel foods at home.
Results and Discussion
The researchers hypothesized that children exposed to a peer model would try more novel foods than children in the no-model condition. The results are summarized in the table below.

Mean Scores by Condition

| Measure | Peer Model Condition | No Model Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Number of foods tasted (out of 4) | 3.1 | 1.4 |
| Grams consumed (mean) | 8.7 | 3.2 |
| Parent diary: requested novel food at home (% yes) | 54% | 21% |

Children in the peer model condition tasted significantly more foods, consumed larger quantities, and were more than twice as likely to later request those foods at home. The researchers concluded that peer modeling is a powerful mechanism for shaping food neophobia in early childhood and that the effects may persist beyond the laboratory setting.

parts.prompt: Identify the research method used in the study.

scoring_points.description: Accurately identifies the research method as an experiment, referencing random assignment or manipulation of an independent variable.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for accurately identifying the research method as an experiment. Acceptable response: This was an experiment because children were randomly assigned to the peer model or no model condition, or because the researchers manipulated the independent variable (presence or absence of a peer model).

sample_responses.response: The researchers used an experiment because children were randomly assigned to either the peer model or no model condition, which is the defining characteristic of experimental research.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the method and supports the identification with the key criterion — random assignment. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: This study is an experiment because the researchers manipulated an independent variable — whether a peer model was present — and observed the effect on how much food the children tasted.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid path emphasizing IV manipulation. Both random assignment and IV manipulation are acceptable justifications. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The researchers used naturalistic observation because they watched children behave around food in a realistic setting.
sample_responses.commentary: Misidentifies the method. Naturalistic observation involves no manipulation of variables and no random assignment — both of which occurred here. The setting was a controlled lab room, not a natural environment. 0 pts.

required_elements: This was an experiment because children were randomly assigned to the peer model or no model condition.
alternatives.correct_example: This was an experiment because children were randomly assigned to the peer model or no model condition.

required_elements: The researchers conducted an experiment by manipulating the independent variable (presence or absence of a peer model) and randomly assigning participants to conditions.
alternatives.correct_example: The researchers conducted an experiment by manipulating the independent variable (presence or absence of a peer model) and randomly assigning participants to conditions.

wrong_examples: The researchers used an observational study. [Incorrect — random assignment and IV manipulation make it an experiment.]
This was a naturalistic observation. [Incorrect — it was a controlled lab experiment.]
common_traps: Confusing an experiment with an observational study because the researchers observed behavior
Identifying the follow-up diary as the main research method

parts.prompt: State the operational definition of willingness to try novel foods as used in the study.

scoring_points.description: States a measurable or quantifiable definition of willingness to try novel foods as operationalized in the study — the number of food items tasted (out of 4) and/or total grams consumed.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for stating the operational definition of willingness to try novel foods as used in the study. Acceptable response: Willingness to try novel foods was operationally defined as the number of food items tasted out of four options, and/or the total grams of food consumed, measured during the three-minute session.

sample_responses.response: In this study, willingness to try novel foods was operationally defined as the number of the four food items a child tasted during the three-minute session, recorded by the experimenter.
sample_responses.commentary: Accurately references the specific measurement (number of foods tasted out of 4) as the operationalization used in the study. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Willingness to try novel foods was operationalized as the total grams of food consumed, measured with a digital scale after the session.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid operationalization from the study — grams consumed was also a primary measure. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Willingness to try new foods means whether a child was open-minded enough to try something unfamiliar without prompting.
sample_responses.commentary: This is a conceptual definition of willingness, not the study's specific operationalization. The question requires citing how the variable was actually measured in the study. 0 pts.

required_elements: The operational definition is the number of novel food items a child tasted out of four options during the three-minute session.
alternatives.correct_example: The operational definition is the number of novel food items a child tasted out of four options during the three-minute session.

required_elements: Willingness to try new foods was operationalized as the number of foods tasted and total grams consumed, recorded by the experimenter after the session.
alternatives.correct_example: Willingness to try new foods was operationalized as the number of foods tasted and total grams consumed, recorded by the experimenter after the session.

wrong_examples: Willingness means whether the child wanted to eat. [Conceptual definition, not the operationalization used.]
Willingness was measured by the parent diary at home. [The diary was a follow-up measure, not the primary operational definition of the DV.]
common_traps: Providing a conceptual definition instead of citing the study's specific measure
Confusing the follow-up diary measure with the primary operational definition

parts.prompt: Describe what the data in the table indicate about the difference in grams consumed between the two conditions.

scoring_points.description: Accurately describes what the means indicate about grams consumed — that children in the peer model condition consumed more food (8.7 g) than those in the no model condition (3.2 g) — AND explains what this difference indicates in the context of the study.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for accurately describing what the data indicate about grams consumed between conditions. Acceptable response: Children in the peer model condition consumed an average of 8.7 grams compared to 3.2 grams in the no model condition, indicating that observing a peer model substantially increased how much novel food children ate.

sample_responses.response: Children in the peer model condition consumed nearly three times as much food (8.7 g) compared to those in the no-model condition (3.2 g). This large difference indicates that seeing a peer eat the novel foods substantially increased how much children were willing to consume.
sample_responses.commentary: Cites both means, notes the relative magnitude of the difference, and explains what it indicates about the effect of peer modeling. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The data show that children who watched a peer model consumed more grams of food (8.7 g vs. 3.2 g), indicating that the presence of a model encouraged children to eat greater amounts of the novel foods.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid phrasing that compares the groups and explains the implication. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The no model group ate less food.
sample_responses.commentary: States a directional result without citing the specific data values or explaining what the difference indicates in the context of the study. 0 pts.

required_elements: On average, children who observed a peer model ate about 2.7 times more food (8.7 g) than those without a model (3.2 g), indicating that peer modeling substantially increased food consumption.
alternatives.correct_example: On average, children who observed a peer model ate about 2.7 times more food (8.7 g) than those without a model (3.2 g), indicating that peer modeling substantially increased food consumption.

required_elements: The peer model condition produced higher food consumption than the no model condition, with means of 8.7 and 3.2 grams respectively, indicating that the presence of a model encouraged children to eat more.
alternatives.correct_example: The peer model condition produced higher food consumption than the no model condition, with means of 8.7 and 3.2 grams respectively, indicating that the presence of a model encouraged children to eat more.

wrong_examples: The peer model group ate more. [No data cited and no explanation of what this indicates.]
The mean of the peer model group was 8.7. [States only the value without indicating what the difference means in context.]
common_traps: Reporting a single mean value without comparing groups
Describing which group consumed more without explaining what this indicates about the effect of the peer model

parts.prompt: Identify at least one ethical guideline the researchers followed in conducting this study.

scoring_points.description: Accurately identifies at least one ethical guideline actually applied in the study, supported by evidence from the study description.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for identifying at least one ethical guideline the researchers actually followed, supported by evidence from the study. Acceptable responses include: written parental informed consent was obtained; children with food allergies were excluded to protect them from harm; or deception via confederate child would require debriefing.

sample_responses.response: The researchers obtained written parental consent for all child participants before the study began, following the ethical guideline of informed consent — which is especially important when participants are minors.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies informed consent, notes the specific form (written parental consent), and explains why it is especially relevant for child participants. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The researchers protected participant welfare by excluding children with known food allergies or developmental delays, ensuring no child would be physically harmed by the procedure.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid path identifying the protection-from-harm principle with specific evidence from the exclusion criteria. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The researchers should have asked the children themselves for consent rather than just the parents.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes a suggestion for improvement, not an ethical guideline that was actually followed. The question asks what the researchers did do ethically. 0 pts.

required_elements: Written parental consent was obtained for all child participants, following the ethical guideline of informed consent for minor participants.
alternatives.correct_example: Written parental consent was obtained for all child participants, following the ethical guideline of informed consent for minor participants.

required_elements: Children with food allergies were excluded from participation, protecting them from potential physical harm.
alternatives.correct_example: Children with food allergies were excluded from participation, protecting them from potential physical harm.

required_elements: The use of a confederate child constituted deception, which would require debriefing of parents and participants at the end of the study.
alternatives.correct_example: The use of a confederate child constituted deception, which would require debriefing of parents and participants at the end of the study.

wrong_examples: The researchers should have gotten consent from the children directly. [Identifies what could have been done rather than what was done.]
common_traps: Identifying an ethical concern rather than a guideline that was followed
Proposing an ethical improvement rather than describing an applied guideline

parts.prompt: Explain the extent to which the research findings may or may not be generalizable, using specific and relevant evidence from the study.
parts.scoring_notes: If students assert the study is 'partly' generalizable, their evidence must support both a reason it is and is not generalizable.

scoring_points.description: Explains the extent to which the study is generalizable with a specific claim about a population AND specific evidence referencing participant variables from the study.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining the extent to which findings may or may not be generalizable, with a specific claim about a population AND supporting evidence from participant characteristics. Acceptable response: The findings may not generalize beyond 4-year-old suburban middle-income children because the sample came from only six preschools in a single school district, or because the sample was predominantly White/European American (61%).

sample_responses.response: The findings may not be generalizable beyond suburban, middle-income preschoolers because all 88 children were recruited from just six preschools in a single school district. Children in urban, rural, or lower-income settings may have different food exposure histories and responses to peer modeling.
sample_responses.commentary: Makes a specific generalizability claim about population and limits, and supports it with specific participant characteristics (six preschools, single district, middle-income). Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The study's findings may have limited generalizability because 61% of the sample identified as White/European American. The peer modeling effect on food neophobia may differ across cultural backgrounds where food norms and social eating practices vary.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid path using the racial composition data as the limiting characteristic. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The study may not be generalizable because it only used 88 children.
sample_responses.commentary: References sample size without explaining which population the findings may or may not apply to. Sample size does not determine generalizability by itself — representativeness does. 0 pts.

required_elements: The findings may not be generalizable beyond 4-year-old suburban middle-income children because the sample was drawn from only six preschool programs in a single school district.
alternatives.correct_example: The findings may not be generalizable beyond 4-year-old suburban middle-income children because the sample was drawn from only six preschool programs in a single school district.

required_elements: The findings may be limited in generalizability because the sample was predominantly White/European American (61%), which may not represent the full population of preschool-aged children.
alternatives.correct_example: The findings may be limited in generalizability because the sample was predominantly White/European American (61%), which may not represent the full population of preschool-aged children.

required_elements: The study may generalize to children of similar age range (3 years 10 months to 4 years 6 months) because the sample was narrowly defined and controlled for developmental stage.
alternatives.correct_example: The study may generalize to children of similar age range (3 years 10 months to 4 years 6 months) because the sample was narrowly defined and controlled for developmental stage.

wrong_examples: The study is not generalizable. [No evidence provided.]
The study had only 88 kids, so it may not generalize. [Describes sample size without referencing which population the findings may or may not apply to.]
common_traps: Asserting generalizability without citing specific participant variables
Claiming partial generalizability without supporting both directions

parts.prompt: Explain how at least one of the research findings supports or refutes the hypothesis that children exposed to a peer model would try more novel foods than children in the no-model condition.
parts.scoring_notes: Partial credit (1 pt) for using evidence without full explanation, or explaining without a specific finding. Full credit (2 pts) requires a specific, accurately interpreted finding AND an explanation of how it supports or refutes the hypothesis.

scoring_points.description: 1 point (partial): Uses specific evidence from the study OR accurately explains the relationship between a finding and the hypothesis, but not both in a fully developed argument.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for using specific evidence from the study OR explaining the relationship between a finding and the hypothesis — not requiring both. Acceptable partial response: The peer model condition had children tasting 3.1 foods versus 1.4 in the no-model condition, which supports the hypothesis.

sample_responses.response: Children in the peer model condition tasted 3.1 foods on average compared to 1.4 in the no-model condition, which supports the hypothesis.
sample_responses.commentary: Cites specific data and notes it supports the hypothesis but does not fully explain why or how the finding connects mechanistically. Earns the partial credit point.

sample_responses.response: The hypothesis was supported because the peer model group did better.
sample_responses.commentary: No specific data cited and no explanation. A vague directional claim without evidence does not earn credit. 0 pts.

required_elements: Uses a result from the table in relation to the hypothesis without a full explanation.
alternatives.correct_example: The peer model condition had children tasting an average of 3.1 foods versus 1.4 in the no-model condition, which supports the hypothesis.

wrong_examples: The hypothesis was supported because the peer model group did better. [No specific data cited.]
common_traps: Citing a result without explaining the connection to the hypothesis
Explaining a connection without referencing specific findings

scoring_points.description: 1 point (full): Uses a specific, accurately interpreted finding AND explains how it supports or refutes the hypothesis that peer modeling increases children's willingness to try novel foods.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for using a specific, accurately interpreted finding AND explaining how that finding supports or refutes the hypothesis. Acceptable response: Children in the peer model condition tasted 3.1 out of 4 foods versus 1.4 in the no-model condition, and consumed more than twice as many grams (8.7 vs. 3.2), supporting the hypothesis that observing a peer model increases willingness to try novel foods.

sample_responses.response: The data strongly support the hypothesis. Children who watched a peer model tasted 3.1 out of 4 foods compared to only 1.4 in the no-model condition, and they consumed nearly three times as many grams (8.7 vs. 3.2). These differences indicate that seeing a peer willingly eat the food directly increased children's willingness to try it themselves — exactly what the observational learning hypothesis predicted.
sample_responses.commentary: Cites multiple specific data points accurately, explicitly explains how each connects to the hypothesis, and identifies the mechanism (observational learning). Earns the full point.

sample_responses.response: The follow-up data support the hypothesis even beyond the lab: 54% of peer-model children later asked for the novel foods at home, compared to only 21% of no-model children. This shows that exposure to a peer model not only increased willingness at the time but also led to lasting preference changes — supporting the hypothesis that peer modeling shapes food neophobia.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid path using the follow-up diary data. Correctly interprets the direction, cites the specific percentages, and explains how the finding supports the hypothesis. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The peer model group tried more foods, which means peer modeling works.
sample_responses.commentary: No specific data cited, no comparison of the two conditions numerically, and no explanation of how the finding connects to the hypothesis. Too vague to earn the full-credit point. 0 pts.

required_elements: Children in the peer model condition tasted an average of 3.1 out of 4 foods, compared to only 1.4 in the no-model condition, and consumed more than twice as many grams (8.7 vs. 3.2) — supporting the hypothesis that observing a peer model increases willingness to try novel foods.
alternatives.correct_example: Children in the peer model condition tasted an average of 3.1 out of 4 foods, compared to only 1.4 in the no-model condition, and consumed more than twice as many grams (8.7 vs. 3.2) — supporting the hypothesis that observing a peer model increases willingness to try novel foods.

required_elements: The follow-up data also support the hypothesis: 54% of peer model children later requested the novel foods at home versus only 21% of no-model children, indicating that the modeling effect persisted beyond the laboratory and increased willingness to try new foods.
alternatives.correct_example: The follow-up data also support the hypothesis: 54% of peer model children later requested the novel foods at home versus only 21% of no-model children, indicating that the modeling effect persisted beyond the laboratory and increased willingness to try new foods.

wrong_examples: The peer model group ate more. [Too vague — no specific data cited and no explanation connecting the finding to the hypothesis.]
common_traps: Vaguely stating the peer model group did better without citing specific data
Citing data without explaining how it specifically supports or refutes the hypothesis
